question:
A game involves jumping to the right on the real number line. If $a$ and $b$ are real numbers
and $b > a$, the cost of jumping from $a$ to $b$ is $b^3-ab^2$. For what real numbers
$c$ can one travel from $0$ to $1$ in a finite number of jumps with total cost exactly $c$?
solution:
The desired real numbers $c$ are precisely those for which $1/3 < c \leq 1$.
For any positive integer $m$ and any
sequence $0 = x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_m = 1$,
the cost of jumping along this sequence is
$\sum_{i=1}^m (x_i - x_{i-1})x_i^2$. Since
\begin{align*}
1 = \sum_{i=1}^m (x_i - x_{i-1}) &\geq \sum_{i=1}^m (x_i - x_{i-1})x_i^2 \\
&> \sum_{i=1}^m \int_{x_i}^{x_{i-1}} t^2\,dt \\
&= \int_0^1 t^2\,dt = \frac{1}{3},
\end{align*}
we can only achieve costs $c$ for which $1/3 < c \leq 1$.

It remains to check that any such $c$ can be achieved.
Suppose $0 = x_0 < \dots < x_m = 1$ is a sequence with $m \geq 1$.
For $i=1,\dots,m$,
let $c_i$ be the cost of the sequence $0, x_i, x_{i+1},\dots,x_m$.
For $i > 1$ and $0 < y \leq x_{i-1}$,
the cost of the sequence $0, y, x_{i}, \dots, x_m$
is
\[
c_{i} + y^3 + (x_i - y)x_i^2 - x_i^3
= c_i - y(x_i^2 - y^2),
\]
which is less than $c_i$ but approaches $c_i$ as $y \to 0$.
By continuity, for $i=2,\dots,m$,
every value in the interval $[c_{i-1}, c_{i})$ can be achieved,
as can $c_m = 1$ by the sequence $0,1$.

To show that all costs $c$ with $1/3 < c \leq 1$ can be achieved, it now suffices
to check that for every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a sequence with cost at most
$1/3 + \epsilon$. For instance, if we take $x_i = i/m$ for $i=0,\dots,m$, the cost
becomes
\[
\frac{1}{m^3} (1^2 + \cdots + m^2)
 = \frac{(m+1)(2m+1)}{6m^2},
\]
which converges to $1/3$ as $m \to +\infty$.

\textbf{Reinterpretation.} The cost of jumping along a particular sequence is an
upper Riemann sum of the function $t^2$. The fact that this function admits a Riemann
integral implies that for any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists $\delta_0$ such that the
cost of the sequence $x_0,\dots,x_m$ is at most $1/3 + \epsilon$ as long as
$\max_i \{x_i - x_{i-1}\} < \epsilon$. (The computation of the integral using the
sequence $x_i = i/m$ was already known to Archimedes.)

descriptive_long.question:
A game involves jumping to the right on the real number line. If $startpoint$ and $endpoint$ are real numbers
and $endpoint > startpoint$, the cost of jumping from $startpoint$ to $endpoint$ is $endpoint^3-startpoint\,endpoint^2$. For what real numbers
$totalcost$ can one travel from $0$ to $1$ in a finite number of jumps with total cost exactly $totalcost$?
descriptive_long.solution:
The desired real numbers $totalcost$ are precisely those for which $\tfrac13 < totalcost \le 1$.
For any positive integer $partition$ and any
sequence $0 = pointzero < pointone < \cdots < pointend = 1$, the cost of jumping along this sequence is
$\displaystyle \sum_{iterator=1}^{partition}(pointvar-pointpre)pointvar^2$. Since
\begin{align*}
1 = \sum_{iterator=1}^{partition}(pointvar-pointpre) &\ge \sum_{iterator=1}^{partition}(pointvar-pointpre)pointvar^2\\
&> \sum_{iterator=1}^{partition}\int_{pointvar}^{pointpre}integrand^2\,d integrand\\
&= \int_0^1 integrand^2\,d integrand = \frac{1}{3},
\end{align*}
we can only achieve costs $totalcost$ for which $\tfrac13 < totalcost \le 1$.

It remains to check that any such $totalcost$ can be achieved.
Suppose $0 = pointzero < \dots < pointend = 1$ is a sequence with $partition \ge 1$.
For $iterator=1,\dots,partition$, let $costindex$ be the cost of the sequence $0, pointvar, pointnext,\dots, pointend$.
For $iterator>1$ and $0 < auxpoint \le pointpre$, the cost of the sequence $0, auxpoint, pointvar, \dots, pointend$ is
\[
costindex + auxpoint^3 + (pointvar-auxpoint)pointvar^2 - pointvar^3
= costindex - auxpoint(pointvar^2 - auxpoint^2),
\]
which is less than $costindex$ but approaches $costindex$ as $auxpoint \to 0$.
By continuity, for $iterator=2,\dots,partition$, every value in the interval $[costprev, costindex)$ can be achieved, as can
$costfinal = 1$ by the sequence $0,1$.

To show that all costs $totalcost$ with $\tfrac13 < totalcost \le 1$ can be achieved, it now suffices to check that for every $accuracy > 0$, there exists a sequence with cost at most $\tfrac13 + accuracy$. For instance, if we take $pointvar = iterator/partition$ for $iterator=0,\dots,partition$, the cost becomes
\[
\frac{1}{partition^3}(1^2+\cdots+partition^2) = \frac{(partition+1)(2partition+1)}{6partition^2},
\]
which converges to $\tfrac13$ as $partition \to +\infty$.

\textbf{Reinterpretation.} The cost of jumping along a particular sequence is an upper Riemann sum of the function $integrand^2$. The fact that this function admits a Riemann integral implies that for any $accuracy > 0$, there exists $threshold$ such that the cost of the sequence $pointzero,\dots,pointend$ is at most $\tfrac13 + accuracy$ as long as $\max_{iterator}(pointvar-pointpre) < accuracy$. (The computation of the integral using the sequence $pointvar = iterator/partition$ was already known to Archimedes.)

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
A game involves jumping to the right on the real number line. If $sunflower$ and $expedition$ are real numbers
and $expedition > sunflower$, the cost of jumping from $sunflower$ to $expedition$ is $expedition^3 - sunflower\,expedition^{2}$. For what real numbers
$starfish$ can one travel from $0$ to $1$ in a finite number of jumps with total cost exactly $starfish$?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The desired real numbers $starfish$ are precisely those for which $1/3 < starfish \leq 1$.
For any positive integer $avalanche$ and any
sequence $0 = thunderstorm < bracelet < \cdots < swallows = 1$,
the cost of jumping along this sequence is
$\sum_{i=1}^{avalanche} (marigold - crocodile)\,marigold^{2}$. Since
\begin{align*}
1 = \sum_{i=1}^{avalanche} (marigold - crocodile) &\geq \sum_{i=1}^{avalanche} (marigold - crocodile)\,marigold^{2} \\
&> \sum_{i=1}^{avalanche} \int_{marigold}^{crocodile} sandcastle^{2}\,d sandcastle \\
&= \int_0^1 sandcastle^{2}\,d sandcastle = \frac{1}{3},
\end{align*}
we can only achieve costs $starfish$ for which $1/3 < starfish \leq 1$.

It remains to check that any such $starfish$ can be achieved.
Suppose $0 = thunderstorm < \dots < swallows = 1$ is a sequence with $avalanche \geq 1$.
For $i=1,\dots,avalanche$, let $lighthouse$ be the cost of the sequence $0, marigold, pinecone,\dots,swallows$.
For $i > 1$ and $0 < sailboat \leq crocodile$, the cost of the sequence $0, sailboat, marigold, \dots, swallows$ is
\[
lighthouse + sailboat^{3} + (marigold - sailboat)\,marigold^{2} - marigold^{3}
= lighthouse - sailboat\,(marigold^{2} - sailboat^{2}),
\]
which is less than $lighthouse$ but approaches $lighthouse$ as $sailboat \to 0$.
By continuity, for $i=2,\dots,avalanche$, every value in the interval $[hurricane, lighthouse)$ can be achieved,
as can $porcupine = 1$ by the sequence $0,1$.

To show that all costs $starfish$ with $1/3 < starfish \leq 1$ can be achieved, it now suffices to check
that for every $asteroid > 0$, there exists a sequence with cost at most $1/3 + asteroid$.
For instance, if we take $marigold = i/avalanche$ for $i=0,\dots,avalanche$, the cost becomes
\[
\frac{1}{avalanche^{3}}(1^{2} + \cdots + avalanche^{2})
= \frac{(avalanche+1)(2\,avalanche+1)}{6\,avalanche^{2}},
\]
which converges to $1/3$ as $avalanche \to +\infty$.

\textbf{Reinterpretation.} The cost of jumping along a particular sequence is an upper Riemann sum of the
function $sandcastle^{2}$. The fact that this function admits a Riemann integral implies that for any $asteroid > 0$, there exists
$gemstone$ such that the cost of the sequence $thunderstorm,\dots,swallows$ is at most $1/3 + asteroid$ as long as
$\max_i \{marigold - crocodile\} < asteroid$. (The computation of the integral using the sequence
$marigold = i/avalanche$ was already known to Archimedes.)

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
A game involves jumping to the right on the real number line. If $terminal$ and $smallest$ are real numbers
and $smallest > terminal$, the cost of jumping from $terminal$ to $smallest$ is $smallest^3-terminal\,smallest^2$. For what real numbers
$windfall$ can one travel from $0$ to $1$ in a finite number of jumps with total cost exactly $windfall$?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The desired real numbers $windfall$ are precisely those for which $1/3 < windfall \leq 1$.
For any positive integer $continuum$ and any
sequence $0 = endpoint < terminus < \cdots < commence = 1$,
the cost of jumping along this sequence is
$\sum_{entirety=1}^{continuum} (destination - originator)destination^2$.
Since
\begin{align*}
1 = \sum_{entirety=1}^{continuum} (destination - originator) &\geq \sum_{entirety=1}^{continuum} (destination - originator)destination^2 \\
&> \sum_{entirety=1}^{continuum} \int_{destination}^{originator} timeless^2\,d timeless \\
&= \int_0^1 timeless^2\,d timeless = \frac{1}{3},
\end{align*}
we can only achieve costs $windfall$ for which $1/3 < windfall \leq 1$.

It remains to check that any such $windfall$ can be achieved.
Suppose $0 = endpoint < \dots < commence = 1$ is a sequence with $continuum \geq 1$.
For $entirety=1,\dots,continuum$,
let $largesse$ be the cost of the sequence $0, destination, departure,\dots,commence$.
For $entirety > 1$ and $0 < vastness \leq originator$,
the cost of the sequence $0, vastness, destination, \dots, commence$ is
\[
largesse + vastness^3 + (destination - vastness)destination^2 - destination^3
= largesse - vastness(destination^2 - vastness^2),
\]
which is less than $largesse$ but approaches $largesse$ as $vastness \to 0$.
By continuity, for $entirety=2,\dots,continuum$,
every value in the interval $[donation, largesse)$ can be achieved,
as can $abundance = 1$ by the sequence $0,1$.

To show that all costs $windfall$ with $1/3 < windfall \leq 1$ can be achieved, it now suffices
to check that for every $gigantic > 0$, there exists a sequence with cost at most
$1/3 + gigantic$. For instance, if we take $destination = entirety/continuum$ for $entirety=0,\dots,continuum$, the cost
becomes
\[
\frac{1}{continuum^3} (1^2 + \cdots + continuum^2)
 = \frac{(continuum+1)(2continuum+1)}{6continuum^2},
\]
which converges to $1/3$ as $continuum \to +\infty$.

\textbf{Reinterpretation.} The cost of jumping along a particular sequence is an upper Riemann sum of the function $timeless^2$. The fact that this function admits a Riemann integral implies that for any $gigantic > 0$, there exists $enormity$ such that the cost of the sequence $endpoint,\dots,commence$ is at most $1/3 + gigantic$ as long as $\max_{entirety} \{destination - originator\} < gigantic$. (The computation of the integral using the sequence $destination = entirety/continuum$ was already known to Archimedes.)

garbled_string.question:
A game involves jumping to the right on the real number line. If $qzxwvtnp$ and $hjgrksla$ are real numbers
and $hjgrksla > qzxwvtnp$, the cost of jumping from $qzxwvtnp$ to $hjgrksla$ is $hjgrksla^3-qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^2$. For what real numbers
$brgmlswz$ can one travel from $0$ to $1$ in a finite number of jumps with total cost exactly $brgmlswz$?
garbled_string.solution:
The desired real numbers $brgmlswz$ are precisely those for which $1/3 < brgmlswz \leq 1$.
For any positive integer $rwkcyhbt$ and any
sequence $0 = afzxsqwe < vklmnqpt < \cdots < scrlivuw = 1$, the cost of jumping along this sequence is
$\sum_{pivchzqe=1}^{rwkcyhbt} (guxsnbre - ojprytcm)guxsnbre^2$. Since
\begin{align*}
1 = \sum_{pivchzqe=1}^{rwkcyhbt} (guxsnbre - ojprytcm) &\geq \sum_{pivchzqe=1}^{rwkcyhbt} (guxsnbre - ojprytcm)guxsnbre^2 \\
&> \sum_{pivchzqe=1}^{rwkcyhbt} \int_{guxsnbre}^{ojprytcm} mlekfaru^2\,d mlekfaru \\
&= \int_0^1 mlekfaru^2\,d mlekfaru = \frac{1}{3},
\end{align*}
we can only achieve costs $brgmlswz$ for which $1/3 < brgmlswz \leq 1$.

It remains to check that any such $brgmlswz$ can be achieved.
Suppose $0 = afzxsqwe < \dots < scrlivuw = 1$ is a sequence with $rwkcyhbt \geq 1$.
For $pivchzqe=1,\dots,rwkcyhbt$, let $kndjvqpo$ be the cost of the sequence $0, guxsnbre, fvdhqazx,\dots,scrlivuw$.
For $pivchzqe > 1$ and $0 < dernmpqc \leq ojprytcm$, the cost of the sequence $0, dernmpqc, guxsnbre, \dots, scrlivuw$ is
\[
kndjvqpo + dernmpqc^3 + (guxsnbre - dernmpqc)guxsnbre^2 - guxsnbre^3
= kndjvqpo - dernmpqc(guxsnbre^2 - dernmpqc^2),
\]
which is less than $kndjvqpo$ but approaches $kndjvqpo$ as $dernmpqc \to 0$.
By continuity, for $pivchzqe=2,\dots,rwkcyhbt$, every value in the interval $[zcsfxeua, kndjvqpo)$ can be achieved, as can $ldowrngi = 1$ by the sequence $0,1$.

To show that all costs $brgmlswz$ with $1/3 < brgmlswz \leq 1$ can be achieved, it now suffices to check that for every $vysqnjlo > 0$, there exists a sequence with cost at most $1/3 + vysqnjlo$.
For instance, if we take $guxsnbre = pivchzqe/rwkcyhbt$ for $pivchzqe=0,\dots,rwkcyhbt$, the cost becomes
\[
\frac{1}{rwkcyhbt^3} (1^2 + \cdots + rwkcyhbt^2)
 = \frac{(rwkcyhbt+1)(2rwkcyhbt+1)}{6rwkcyhbt^2},
\]
which converges to $1/3$ as $rwkcyhbt \to +\infty$.

\textbf{Reinterpretation.} The cost of jumping along a particular sequence is an upper Riemann sum of the function $mlekfaru^2$.
The fact that this function admits a Riemann integral implies that for any $vysqnjlo > 0$, there exists $cakvzerw$ such that the cost of the sequence $afzxsqwe,\dots,scrlivuw$ is at most $1/3 + vysqnjlo$ as long as $\max_{pivchzqe} \{guxsnbre - ojprytcm\} < vysqnjlo$.
(The computation of the integral using the sequence $guxsnbre = pivchzqe/rwkcyhbt$ was already known to Archimedes.)

kernel_variant.question:
A game is played on the real number line.  For real numbers a<b the (right-hand) jump from a to b costs

        cost(a\to b)=b^4-ab^3.

Starting at 0 you must reach 2 by a finite sequence of right-hand jumps

        0=x_0<x_1<\cdots <x_m=2.

For which real numbers c can the total cost

        C(x_0,x_1,\ldots ,x_m)=\sum _{i=1}^{m}cost(x_{i-1}\to x_i)

be made exactly equal to c ?
kernel_variant.solution:
Answer.  A total cost can be realised exactly when
                    4 < c \leq  16.

Proof.

1.  Universal lower and upper bounds
   ---------------------------------
   For a single jump x_{i-1}\to x_i we have
        cost(x_{i-1}\to x_i)=x_i^4-x_{i-1}x_i^3=(x_i-x_{i-1})x_i^3.
   Therefore
        C=\sum _{i=1}^{m}(x_i-x_{i-1})x_i^3.             (1)

   Because t\mapsto t^3 is increasing on [0,2], the right-hand sum in (1) is an
   upper Riemann sum for \int _0^2t^3dt; hence
        C > \int _0^2 t^3dt = 4.                              (2)

   On the other hand x_i\leq 2, so each term in (1) is at most
        (x_i-x_{i-1})\cdot 2^3 = 8(x_i-x_{i-1}),
   which gives
        C \leq  8\cdot (2-0)=16.                               (3)
   Thus every attainable cost satisfies
                      4 < C \leq  16.                    (\star )

2.  A family of partitions that attains every value in (4,16]
   ---------------------------------------------------------
   Fix an integer N\geq 2 and a parameter k with 0<k\leq 2.  Consider the partition
        P_N(k): 0, k, k+h, k+2h,\ldots ,k+Nh = 2,
   where h=(2-k)/N.  There are N+1 jumps, the first of length k and the
   remaining N jumps all of length h.  Let
        C_N(k) = k^4 + \sum _{j=1}^{N} h (k+jh)^3.          (4)
   Plainly k\mapsto C_N(k) is continuous on [0,2].

   End-values.  Directly from (4)
        C_N(2)=2^4=16.                                 (5)
   Taking k\to 0^+ while keeping N fixed gives
        C_N(0^+)=4\left(1+\dfrac{1}{N}\right)^{2}.      (6)
   This number exceeds 4 for every N.

   A value strictly smaller than C_N(0^+).  What we really need is a single
   point where C_N drops below the value in (6).  Set k=2/N (note that
   k>0 because N\geq 2).  Elementary but careful algebra based on (4) yields

        C_N\!\left(\tfrac{2}{N}\right)
            =4+\frac{8}{N}-\frac{4}{N^{2}}-\frac{8}{N^{3}}+\frac{4}{N^{4}}
             +\frac{16}{N^{5}}-\frac{4}{N^{6}}.        (7)

   Compare this with (6):
        C_N(0^+)-C_N\!\left(\tfrac{2}{N}\right)
          =  \frac{8}{N^{2}}+\frac{8}{N^{3}}-\frac{4}{N^{4}}-\frac{16}{N^{5}}+\frac{4}{N^{6}}
          =  \frac{8N^{4}+8N^{3}-4N^{2}-16N+4}{N^{6}}. (8)
   The numerator in (8) is 8N^{4}+8N^{3}-4N^{2}-16N+4>0 for every N\geq 2,
   so indeed
        C_N\!\left(\tfrac{2}{N}\right) < C_N(0^+).      (9)
   Consequently the image of the continuous map k\mapsto C_N(k) is an interval
        [m_N,16] with 4<m_N<C_N(0^+).                   (10)

   Approximating an arbitrary c\in (4,16].
   ------------------------------------
   Given c with 4<c\leq 16, choose N so large that
        C_N(0^+)=4(1+1/N)^{2}<c.                        (11)
   Because m_N<c\leq 16 and m_N\leq C_N(0^+) by (10), the value c lies inside the
   image interval [m_N,16].  By continuity there exists k\in (0,2] such that
        C_N(k)=c.                                      (12)
   The partition P_N(k) therefore realises the cost c.

3.  Conclusion
   -----------
   Part 1 showed that every attainable cost lies in (4,16];
   Part 2 showed that every number in that interval is attainable.
   Hence the set of attainable costs is exactly (4,16], as claimed. \blacksquare

core_steps: Rewrite each jump cost as (b−a)·b², so the total cost for 0=x₀<⋯<x_m=1 is Σ (x_i−x_{i−1})x_i² — an upper Riemann sum for f(t)=t² on [0,1].
Because t² is increasing, ∫₀¹ t²dt < Σ (x_i−x_{i−1})x_i² ≤ Σ (x_i−x_{i−1})·1 = 1, giving 1/3 < cost ≤ 1.
Uniform partitions with mesh →0 (e.g., x_i=i/m) make the upper sum arbitrarily close to 1/3 from above.
Moving the first (or any) breakpoint continuously varies the cost, filling every value between two attainable sums; inductively this produces all costs in (1/3,1].

slot1.description: Final point of the journey (currently the target right-endpoint of the interval).

slot2.description: Power of b in the cost formula: jumps have cost (b−a)·b^k (here k=2, coming from b³−ab²).
